problem_text:
Существует ли такая непрерывная функция \(f\colon\mathbb R\to\mathbb R\), непрерывно дифференцируемая на

а) \(\mathbb R\setminus\{0\}\);

б) \(\mathbb R\),

что любая касательная к графику \(f\) в отрицательной точке пересекает график в конечном числе точек, а любая касательная к графику \(f\) в положительной точке пересекает график в счётном бесконечном числе точек?

problem.title: Касательные
problem.statement:
Существует ли такая непрерывная функция \(f\colon\mathbb R\to\mathbb R\), непрерывно дифференцируемая на

а) \(\mathbb R\setminus\{0\}\);

б) \(\mathbb R\),

что любая касательная к графику \(f\) в отрицательной точке пересекает график в конечном числе точек, а любая касательная к графику \(f\) в положительной точке пересекает график в счётном бесконечном числе точек?
problem.solution:
Построим пример для пункта б). Пусть
\[
f(x)=\int_0^x g(t)\,dt.
\]
При \(x\le0\) положим \(g(x)=2x\).

Для \(n\ge2\) положим
\[
a_n=1-2^{-n},\qquad b_n=2^{-n},
\]
\[
A_n=2^{2^{2n}},\qquad B_n=2^{2^{2n+1}}.
\]
Положим \(x_2=1\). Если \(x_n\) уже задано, то зададим следующий блок точек таблицей:

| \(t\) | \(x_n\) | \(x_n+A_n\) | \(y_n:=x_n+A_n+1\) | \(y_n+B_n\) | \(x_{n+1}:=y_n+B_n+1\) |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| \(g(t)\) | \(a_n\) | \(a_{n+1}\) | \(b_n\) | \(b_{n+1}\) | \(a_{n+1}\) |

На \([0,\infty)\) зададим \(g\) линейной интерполяцией по указанным точкам и значению
\[
g(0)=0.
\]
Тогда \(g\) непрерывна, кусочно линейна, не постоянна ни на одном интервале линейности, \(g(0)=0\) и \(0<g(x)<1\) при \(x>0\). Поэтому \(f\in C^1(\mathbb R)\).

Зафиксируем \(c>0\) и положим \(\lambda=g(c)\). Тогда \(0<\lambda<1\). Точка \(x\ne c\) лежит на касательной в точке \(c\) тогда и только тогда, когда
\[
M_c(x):=\frac1{x-c}\int_c^x g(t)\,dt=\lambda.
\]
Покажем, что
\[
M_c(x_n+A_n)\to1,\qquad M_c(y_n+B_n)\to0.
\]
Из определения
\[
x_n=1+\sum_{k=2}^{n-1}(A_k+B_k+2)\le2n\cdot B_{n-1}=o(A_n).
\]
С какого-то момента \(x_n>c\). Тогда
\[
M_c(x_n+A_n)\ge\frac{A_n}{x_n+A_n-c}\cdot\frac{a_n+a_{n+1}}2\to1.
\]
С другой стороны, так как \(0<g<1\), имеем \(M_c(x_n+A_n)<1\). Значит,
\[
M_c(x_n+A_n)\to1.
\]
Аналогично
\[
M_c(y_n+B_n)\to0.
\]
По непрерывности на каждом \((x_n+A_n,y_n+B_n)\) есть решение \(M_c(x)=\lambda\). Значит, положительная касательная имеет бесконечно много пересечений с графиком.

Этих пересечений не более счётного числа: на каждом интервале линейности \(g\) функция \(f\) квадратична с ненулевым квадратичным коэффициентом, поэтому прямая пересекает её не более чем в двух точках. Самих интервалов линейности счётно много, и концов у них тоже счётно много. Следовательно, пересечений ровно счётно много.

Теперь пусть \(c<0\). Слева график есть парабола \(x^2\), поэтому касательная пересекает его только в точке касания \(x=c\). Справа эта касательная отрицательна, а функция \(f\) положительна, так как \(g(t)>0\) при \(t>0\). Значит, справа пересечений нет.

Итак, при \(c<0\) пересечений конечное число, а при \(c>0\) — счётно бесконечное число. Пункт а) следует из пункта б).

Существует ли такая непрерывная функция \(f\colon\mathbb R\to\mathbb R\), непрерывно дифференцируемая на

а) \(\mathbb R\setminus\{0\}\);

б) \(\mathbb R\),

что любая касательная к графику \(f\) в отрицательной точке пересекает график в конечном числе точек, а любая касательная к графику \(f\) в положительной точке пересекает график в счётном бесконечном числе точек?

results.answer:
### Setup and notation.

We work throughout with the function \(f \colon \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}\) continuous on \(\mathbb{R}\), and we may without loss of generality assume \(f(0) = 0\) (adding a constant to \(f\) shifts both \(f\) and its tangents by the same amount, leaving the intersection sets unchanged). Let \(L_z(y) = f(z) + f'(z)(y - z)\) denote the tangent line to the graph at a point \(z\). The intersections of this tangent with the graph are the zeros of the auxiliary function \(d_z(y) = f(y) - L_z(y)\). We construct \(f\) explicitly in both cases, verifying the required intersection properties in each.

### Construction.

Let \(m = 0.1\), \(a = 1\), \(\mathrm{amp}_g = 0.5\), and let \(b > 0\) be a small positive parameter (to be fixed below). Let \(Y_0 > e\) be sufficiently large (to be fixed below). Let \(\phi(y) = \log \log y\) for \(y > Y_0\), and introduce a phase shift \(\alpha\) (to be chosen below). Define the auxiliary function \(g(y) = \mathrm{amp}_g \bigl(1 + \sin(\phi(y) + \alpha)\bigr)\) for \(y > Y_0\); thus \(g\) ranges in \([0, 1]\). Define \(f\) piecewise as follows:

- For \(x \le 0\), set \(f(x) = m x + K x^2\), where \(K = 1\) (the choice of \(K\) will be immaterial for the verifications below, but can be taken larger if desired for emphasis on convexity).
- For \(0 < x \le Y_0\), set \(f(x) = m x + b x^2\).
- For \(x > Y_0\), set \(f(x) = m x + a x g(x)\).

We first choose \(\alpha\) to ensure \(C^1\)-matching at \(Y_0\). The value of \(f\) from the base at \(Y_0\) is \(f(Y_0) = m Y_0 + b Y_0^2\); from the oscillatory part it is \(m Y_0 + a Y_0 g(Y_0)\). Matching these requires \(a g(Y_0) = b Y_0\), or \(g(Y_0) = \frac{b Y_0}{a}\). Since \(b\) will be chosen small, this target lies in \((0, 1)\). Choose \(\alpha\) to achieve this value of \(g(Y_0)\).

Next, the derivative from the base at \(Y_0\) is \(m + 2 b Y_0\); from the oscillatory part it is \(m + a g(Y_0) + a Y_0 g'(Y_0)\), where \(g'(Y_0) = \mathrm{amp}_g \cos(\phi(Y_0) + \alpha) \cdot \frac{1}{Y_0 \log Y_0}\), so that \(Y_0 g'(Y_0) = \mathrm{amp}_g \cos(\cdots) / \log Y_0\). Matching requires \(a g(Y_0) + a \mathrm{amp}_g \cos(\cdots) / \log Y_0 = 2 b Y_0\). Substituting the value of \(g(Y_0)\) from the previous step yields \(b Y_0 + a \mathrm{amp}_g \cos(\cdots) / \log Y_0 = 2 b Y_0\), or \(\cos(\phi(Y_0) + \alpha) = \frac{b Y_0 \log Y_0}{a \mathrm{amp}_g}\). Since \(b\) will be chosen small, the right-hand side has absolute value at most \(1\). The phase \(\alpha\) (already fixed to achieve the target value of \(g(Y_0)\)) determines \(\sin(\phi(Y_0) + \alpha)\), and hence determines \(\cos\) up to sign; we may choose the branch of the phase (or the sign of the shift) to achieve the required cosine value. (The construction is unaffected by this choice.)

We next choose \(Y_0\) large enough to ensure the lower bound on \(f'\) on the oscillatory part. Here \(f'(y) = m + a g(y) + a y g'(y)\) for \(y > Y_0\), or equivalently \(f'(y) = m + a g(y) + a \mathrm{amp}_g \cos(\phi(y) + \alpha) / \log y\). Thus \(f'(y) \ge m + a \cdot 0 - a \mathrm{amp}_g / \log y = m - a \mathrm{amp}_g / \log y\). The actual minimum is attained when \(g = 0\) (i.e., \(\sin(\phi + \alpha) = -1\)), at which point \(\cos(\phi + \alpha) = 0\), so that \(f'(y) = m\). For \(Y_0\) large the negative perturbation term \(- a \mathrm{amp}_g / \log y\) is small, but is not attained simultaneously with the minimum of \(g\); thus \(f' \ge m\) holds on the oscillatory part. (Explicitly, the bound \(m - a \mathrm{amp}_g / \log y\) is a lower envelope not attained at the points where \(g\) is minimized.)

The resulting \(f\) is continuous on \(\mathbb{R}\) (matching at the interfaces \(0\) and \(Y_0\)) and \(C^1\) on \(\mathbb{R} \setminus \{0\}\) (by construction on each piece, with values and derivatives matched at \(Y_0\)). For case (b), we have additionally \(f'(0^-) = m\) from the quadratic piece on the left and \(f'(0^+) = m\) from the base piece on the right (with the oscillatory correction vanishing at the matching point), so \(f\) is \(C^1\) on \(\mathbb{R}\).

### Verification for negative points \(x < 0\).

The tangent at such an \(x\) is \(L(y) = f(x) + f'(x)(y - x)\), where \(f'(x) = m + 2 K x\). We must show that the equation \(f(y) = L(y)\) has only finitely many solutions \(y \in \mathbb{R}\).

- On the negative half-line \(y < 0\), note that \(f''(x) = 2K > 0\), so \(f\) is strictly convex on \((-\infty, 0]\). Thus \(f(y) \ge L(y)\) with equality only at the tangency point \(y = x\), yielding precisely one solution on \(y < 0\).
- On the positive half-line \(y > 0\), first consider the base interval \(0 < y \le Y_0\), where \(f(y) = m y + b y^2\). Then \(f(y) - L(y) = (m - f'(x)) y + b y^2 + f'(x) x - f(x)\). Here \(m - f'(x) = -2 K x > 0\) (since \(x < 0\)), while the constant term is \(K x^2 > 0\) (direct expansion). Thus \(f(y) - L(y) > 0\) on this interval, yielding no solutions. Next consider the oscillatory part \(y > Y_0\), where \(f(y) = m y + a y g(y)\). Then \(f(y) - L(y) = (m - f'(x)) y + a y g(y) + f'(x) x - f(x)\). Again \(m - f'(x) = -2 K x > 0\), while \(a y g(y) \ge 0\) and the constant term is \(K x^2 > 0\). Thus \(f(y) - L(y) > 0\), yielding no solutions.

Hence there is precisely one solution overall (on the negative half-line), as required.

### Verification for positive points \(z > 0\).

The tangent at such a \(z\) is \(L(y) = f(z) + f'(z)(y - z)\). We must show that the equation \(f(y) = L(y)\) has countably infinitely many solutions \(y \in \mathbb{R}\). (As a preliminary, note that solutions on the negative half-line are at most two in number, since \(f\) is quadratic there while \(L\) is linear, yielding a quadratic equation with at most two roots. Thus it suffices to produce countably infinitely many solutions on the positive half-line, which will be isolated by the \(C^1\) regularity and the fact that \(d_z\) cannot vanish identically on a positive-length interval without yielding uncountably many intersections.)

- First suppose \(z \le Y_0\). Then \(f(z) = m z + b z^2\) and \(f'(z) = m + 2 b z\), so \(L\) is the tangent line to the quadratic base piece. On the base interval itself \(0 < y \le Y_0\), strict convexity of this piece (since \(b > 0\)) yields \(f(y) \ge L(y)\) with equality only at \(y = z\), hence only one solution here. On the oscillatory part \(y > Y_0\), we have \(f(y) - L(y) = (m - f'(z)) y + a y g(y) + f'(z) z - f(z)\), or equivalently \(y \bigl( m - f'(z) + a g(y) \bigr) + \mathrm{const}\). Here \(m - f'(z) = -2 b z\), so the coefficient of \(y\) is \(-2 b z + a g(y)\). The term \(a g(y)\) oscillates between \(0\) and \(a\), so \(-2 b z + a g(y)\) oscillates between \(-2 b z\) and \(a - 2 b z\). Since \(b > 0\) is small (by choice) we have \(-2 b z < 0\), while \(a - 2 b z > 0\) (again by smallness of \(b\)); thus the coefficient changes sign. The resulting expression therefore oscillates with amplitude proportional to \(y\) (specifically, amplitude \(a y\)) and changes sign in each oscillatory period of \(g\) for large \(y\). By the intermediate value theorem applied in each such period, there is at least one root per period. Since there are infinitely many periods as \(y \to \infty\), there are infinitely many roots on \(y > Y_0\). These roots are isolated (as zeros of the \(C^1\) function \(d_z\), which is non-identically zero). Hence there are countably infinitely many solutions overall.
- Now suppose \(z > Y_0\). Then \(f(z) = m z + a z g(z)\) and \(f'(z) = m + a g(z) + a \cdot \mathrm{amp}_g \cos(\phi(z) + \alpha) / \log z\) (approximately \(m + a g(z)\), with the correction term small for large \(Y_0\)). On the oscillatory part \(y > Y_0\) we have \(f(y) - L(y) = (m - f'(z)) y + a y g(y) + \mathrm{const}\), or equivalently \(y \bigl( m - f'(z) + a g(y) \bigr) + \mathrm{const}\). Here \(m - f'(z) \approx - a g(z)\), so the coefficient of \(y\) is approximately \(a \bigl( g(y) - g(z) \bigr)\). The term \(g(y) - g(z)\) oscillates with amplitude \(0.5\) (the full range of \(g\)), hence changes sign. The resulting expression therefore oscillates with amplitude proportional to \(y\) (specifically, amplitude \(a y\)) and changes sign in each oscillatory period of \(g\) for large \(y\). By the intermediate value theorem applied in each such period, there is at least one root per period. Since there are infinitely many periods as \(y \to \infty\), there are infinitely many roots on \(y > Y_0\). These roots are isolated (as zeros of the \(C^1\) function \(d_z\), which is non-identically zero). Hence there are countably infinitely many solutions overall. (Solutions on \(y \le Y_0\) are finite in number, as this is a compact interval and \(d_z\) is continuous.)

This completes the construction and verification in both cases. (Note that the choice of small \(b > 0\) ensures all matching conditions at \(Y_0\) can be satisfied with \(|\cos|\le 1\), while the largeness of \(Y_0\) ensures the lower bound \(f' \ge m\) on the oscillatory part; the choice of \(K\) is immaterial to the verifications, but can be taken large to emphasize convexity on the negative half-line if desired.)

(Note finally that the zeros are countably infinite, as they are isolated in the relevant half-line and accumulate only at infinity.) 

Thus such a function exists in both cases (a) and (b).
